elements.sent-2411-001: Large Language Model (LLM) agents are rapidly improving to handle increasingly complex web-based tasks.
elements.sent-2411-002: Most of these agents rely on general-purpose, proprietary models like GPT-4 and focus on designing better prompts to improve their planning abilities.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, general-purpose LLMs are not specifically trained to understand specialized web contexts such as HTML, and they often struggle with long-horizon planning.
elements.sent-2411-004: We explore an alternative approach that fine-tunes open-source LLMs using production-scale workflow data collected from over 250 domains corresponding to 6 billion tokens.
elements.sent-2411-005: This simple yet effective approach shows substantial gains over prompting-based agents on existing benchmarks---\ouragent achieves state-of-the-art direct generation performance on Mind2Web and improves the task success rate by 14.1\% over the previous best text-only web agents on WebArena.
elements.sent-2411-006: We further perform detailed ablation studies on various fine-tuning design choices and provide insights into LLM selection, training recipes, context window optimization, and effect of dataset sizes.
elements.sent-2411-007: Research lead: Junhong; Engineering leads: Atishay and Zedian.
elements.sent-2411-008: Large language model (LLM) agents have advanced significantly in web navigation.
elements.sent-2411-009: They can carry out user-specified tasks in multiple steps by reasoning on their own what actions to take and what external resources to interface with.
elements.sent-2411-010: Recent studies~\citep{zheng2024synapse,lai2024autowebglm, zhang2024webpilot have shown that, with better planning and exploration strategies, LLM agents can independently solve various web tasks ranging from simple navigation, such as locating a specific Wikipedia page, to more complex operations, such as booking flights or restaurants.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Despite these improvements, the performance of existing web agents on research benchmarks remains significantly below human levels~\citep{deng2023mindweb, zhou2024webarena, drouin2024workarena .}
elements.sent-2411-012: One possible reason is their dependence on general-purpose LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-013: Indeed, all top-performing agents like WebPilot~\citep{zhang2024webpilot , AWM~\citep{awm2024wang , and SteP~\citep{sodhi2024step rely on prompting proprietary models like GPT-4~\citep{gpt4 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: These general-purpose LLMs are not optimized for interpreting web contexts such as HTML or accessibility trees; their pretraining and alignment processes do not address navigation-related challenges; and their proprietary nature presents a major obstacle in adapting them to web environments via continual training.
elements.sent-2411-015: In this work, we explore an alternative approach by fine-tuning open-source LLMs with a large set of real-world workflow data\footnote{Due to privacy concerns, we restrict access to our proprietary dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-016: However, we release our complete preprocessing, training, and inference code, along with a version of \ouragent trained on open-source datasets~\citep{deng2023mindweb on GitHub.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Through extensive experiments, we show that this approach not only boosts the web understanding and planning abilities of LLMs, achieving state-of-the-art results on various benchmarks, but also allows us to develop agent models significantly smaller than proprietary LLMs, reducing the serving costs.
elements.sent-2411-018: Our empirical results suggest that large-scale, high-quality, real-world data can be essential to agent development.
elements.sent-2411-019: Specifically, we collect a set of proprietary workflow data representing action sequences executed by real users in real web environments through \href{https://scribehow.com/ {Scribe .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: This dataset encompasses a large and diverse spectrum of websites (over 250 domains and 10,000 subdomains), task objectives, task difficulty, and task length.
elements.sent-2411-021: Each step in the workflow features not only the raw HTML-DOM of the website but also a comprehensive documentation of the action, including action description in natural language, mouse or keyboard operation, and the CSS selector of the target HTML element.
elements.sent-2411-022: We reformat the data into a next-step prediction formulation and fine-tune a set of open-source LLMs via the parameter-efficient LoRA~\citep{hu2022lora .}
elements.sent-2411-023: After preprocessing and reformatting, our training dataset contains more than 6 billion tokens.

elements.sent-2411-024: With access to this production-scale dataset, we develop \ouragent, the first family of specialized, single-stage LLM agents capable of directly generating the next step based on the website's DOM and action history.
elements.sent-2411-025: This is in contrast with previous fine-tuned agents that require multiple stages to produce an action, e.g., first narrowing down to a set of target element candidates and then selecting one from the candidates~\citep{deng2023mindweb .}
elements.sent-2411-026: To evaluate the capacity and generalization ability of \ouragent, we test it on public benchmarks without any further training.
elements.sent-2411-027: Notably, the 32B-parameter \ouragent-Large achieves state-of-the-art direct generation performance on Mind2Web~\citep{deng2023mindweb , with step success rate surpassing the baselines by 5-10\% across all test sets.}
elements.sent-2411-028: On the end-to-end task execution benchmark WebArena~\citep{zhou2024webarena , our 7B \ouragent-Small improves the previous best task success rate from 37.2\% to 51.3\%, marking the highest performance among text-only LLM agents.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Beyond the empirical results, our work also provides several insights valuable for future web agent research: (1) we show that direct fine-tuning on highly structured inputs (HTML-DOM) is feasible and can improve the agent's ability in identifying the correct target; (2) we identify an effective HTML preprocessing strategy that balances between preserving essential information and minimizing context length; (3) we provide a thorough analysis on various design choices in fine-tuning, such as LLM backbone and context window selection; (4) we illustrate how fine-tuning improves agent performance as dataset size increases.
elements.sent-2411-030: Our work highlights the potential of building web agents via specialized fine-tuning with production-scale data.
elements.sent-2411-031: This approach not only improves agents' capabilities relative to prompt-engineered alternatives, but also reduces inference costs due to the smaller sizes of open-source LLMs.

elements.sent-2411-033: We view \ouragent as an important step towards developing AI assistants and fully automated agents for real-world web applications.

elements.sent-2411-059: In this section, we first overview the general setup of solving web-based tasks with LLM agents.
elements.sent-2411-060: Then, we detail our proposed method to develop specialized agents from open-source LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-061: We consider solving a web-based task as a sequential decision-making process guided by a high-level objective.
elements.sent-2411-062: For each task, the user first specifies an objective and a starting web page.
elements.sent-2411-063: Then, at every step, the agent outputs an action based on the task objective, the current web page, and the history.
elements.sent-2411-064: Formally, denote the user objective as $q$.
elements.sent-2411-065: The web environment is governed by a transition function $T$ that can evolve over time.
elements.sent-2411-066: The agent is instantiated by a language model $L$.
elements.sent-2411-067: At each time step $t$, the agent observes $o_t$ produced by the environment state $s_t$ and observes the history $h_t = H(o_{1:t-1 , a_{1:t-1 )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-068: It outputs an action $a_t = L(q, o_t, h_t)$, which is executed in the environment, and the state changes correspondingly $s_{t+1 =T(s_t, a_t)$.}
elements.sent-2411-069: This iterative process stops when the agent issues a stop signal, or a task termination condition is met, such as we have reached a predefined maximum number of steps.
elements.sent-2411-070: For single-modal, text-only agents, the observation $o_t$ typically consists of the website's URL, the HTML-DOM (Object Model for HTML, which defines HTML elements and their properties, methods, and events), and potentially the accessibility tree (a representation that can be understood by assistive technologies like screen readers).
elements.sent-2411-071: Since the raw HTML-DOM is often long and contains redundant structural information, most methods employ preprocessing and pruning strategies, which could be as simple as retaining a fixed set of HTML tags and attributes or more complex ones like LLM-based element ranking and filtering~\citep{deng2023mindweb .}
elements.sent-2411-072: The action $a_t$ emulates the keyboard and mouse operations available on web pages.
elements.sent-2411-073: The most general action space in existing work consists of element operations, such as clicking, typing, and key combination pressing; tab actions, such as opening, closing, and switching between tabs; navigation actions, such as going forward and backward in the browsing history~\citep{zhou2024webarena .}
elements.sent-2411-074: As discussed earlier, previous web agent work focuses on presenting useful demonstrations through $h_t$ or iteratively revising $a_t$ to improve the quality of the predicted next step.
elements.sent-2411-075: In contrast, we explore whether we can improve the model $L$ itself by learning from a vast amount of data and incorporating more information into $o_t$, such as the natural language description and HTML representation of a action.
elements.sent-2411-076: We detail our approach in the next section.
elements.sent-2411-077: We collected a large set of real-world, user-annotated, proprietary data through \href{https://scribehow.com/ {Scribe , a software that streamlines the creation of step-by-step guides for web-based tasks.}}
elements.sent-2411-078: Scribe allows users to record their interactions with the web through a browser extension and converts the interactions into well-annotated instructions, which can be then customized to specific business needs.
elements.sent-2411-079: The collected dataset consists of everyday workflows in common web application domains, encompassing customer relationship management (CRM) tools like HubSpot and Salesforce; productivity tools like Notion and Calendley; social platforms like Facebook and LinkedIn; shopping sites like Amazon and Shopify; and many others.
elements.sent-2411-080: Each workflow features a high-level user objective and a step-by-step documentation of the action sequence to achieve the task.
elements.sent-2411-081: The objective spans a wide range of topics, such as ``add a user in a Salesforce" or ``invite someone to manage Facebook ad accounts".
elements.sent-2411-082: Each step contains the following information: the current web page's URL, raw HTML-DOM, a natural language description of the action performed, the type of action, and the autogenerated CSS selector to identify the action target.
elements.sent-2411-083: There are three types of actions in the dataset:.
elements.sent-2411-084: Note that there is no scroll actions in our action space since all elements are already fully accessible in the captured data.
elements.sent-2411-085: This is because we capture the full DOM from a system perspective, which inherently includes the entire webpage as observed from the backend.
elements.sent-2411-086: This method differs from user-centric data collection, where only the elements within the visible browser viewport are captured.
elements.sent-2411-087: To ensure the quality of the data, we remove workflows with invalid selectors, i.e., the selector cannot be used to locate a target element in the DOM.
elements.sent-2411-088: We also remove non-English workflows to reduce dataset complexity and enable us to explore English-only LLMs like Mistral 7B~\citep{mistral7b .}
elements.sent-2411-089: The resulting dataset is at production scale: using raw data collected over a two-month period, we are able to extract workflow data from more than 250 domains and 10,000 subdomains with an average task length of 11 steps, which correspond to about 6 billion training tokens.
elements.sent-2411-090: This large-scale, high-quality, real-world dataset is unmatched in prior web agent research.
elements.sent-2411-091: Since this dataset is collected from real users and might contain sensitive and confidential information, it will not be released to the public to protect user privacy.
elements.sent-2411-092: The dataset is solely for research purposes and has been anonymized to prevent the identification of any individual.
elements.sent-2411-093: For \ouragent, we consider an observation space consisting mainly of the URL and HTML-DOM.
elements.sent-2411-094: Specifically, HTML-DOM provides agents with all structural and content information about the web page that are essential for generating the next step and long-term planning.
elements.sent-2411-095: For instance, while a drop-down menu may not be visible on the website before expansion, the agent can detect the menu items from the DOM and determine whether to click and expand it.
elements.sent-2411-096: We do not use accessibility tree to develop \ouragent because it may lose information about the HTML elements, such as the drop-down items, and does not generalize across different browsers and devices.
elements.sent-2411-097: Given our observation space, a subsequent problem is that the DOM can be quite long and exceed the context window of prevailing open-source LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-098: To reduce the DOM sizes, we propose a pruning algorithm that maintains the essential structure and content while eliminating redundant or disruptive elements that could hinder the LLM's understanding.
elements.sent-2411-099: Specifically, we first use the BeautifulSoup library~\citep{richardson2007beautiful to remove non-essential components such as metadata, CSS, and JavaScript.}
elements.sent-2411-100: Then, we utilize a tag-attribute white list to retain useful tag level information like retaining interactive elements.
elements.sent-2411-101: Since some attribute values can contain random character sequences that do not provide useful information, we propose a novel detection method that removes the attributes with character-to-token-ratio smaller than 2, i.e., $\frac{len(s) {len(tokenizer(s)) <2$, where $s$ denotes the value string.}}
elements.sent-2411-102: Intuitively, if each character in a string is encoded using a separate token, it is highly likely that the string is not semantically meaningful.
elements.sent-2411-103: Lastly, we remove the comments and extra whitespaces to clean up the DOM.
elements.sent-2411-104: After pruning, we assign each tag in the HTML with a unique ID by traversing the HTML tree from bottom to top.
elements.sent-2411-105: More details about preprocessing and analysis on the tokenizer-pruning method can be found in Appendix~\ref{appendix:preprocess .}
elements.sent-2411-106: We restrict the action space of \ouragent to the three types of operations specified in Section~\ref{sec:datacollection .}
elements.sent-2411-107: To preprocess the action sequences, we rewrite each step into five lines as follows: \fbox{ \parbox{0.95\textwidth { 1.}}}
elements.sent-2411-108: Description: Click the ``Menu" button to browse all food options Action: mouse\_click\_action Node: 832 Target: $<$svg class=``open-hamburger-icon" node=``832" role=``img"$>$ The first line represents the current time step.
elements.sent-2411-109: The second line is the natural language description of the action, which can help LLMs to learn about the rationale behind applying a specific action.
elements.sent-2411-110: The third line is one of the three operations in the action space.
elements.sent-2411-111: The fourth line is the unique ID assigned to the target element.
elements.sent-2411-112: The last line details the HTML tag and attributes, which can be directly obtained from the processed DOM.
elements.sent-2411-113: For the history, we consider only previous actions, omitting previous observations due to the extensive length of the DOMs.
elements.sent-2411-115: Therefore, at each step, \ouragent will be given the task objective, URL, HTML-DOM, and all previous actions in the aforementioned five-line format.
elements.sent-2411-116: Its goal is to output the next action $a_t = L(q, o_t, a_{1:t-1 )$ that helps complete the task.}
elements.sent-2411-117: In Appendix~\ref{appendix:exampleworkflow , we provide an example of a full workflow.}
elements.sent-2411-118: Lastly, during our inspection, we find that 10\% of the action descriptions in the dataset are not informative (e.g., ``click here").
elements.sent-2411-119: In these cases, we use GPT-4o~\citep{gpt4o to regenerate the action description from screenshots.}
elements.sent-2411-120: We provide the prompt as well as examples of the regenerated action descriptions in Appendix~\ref{app:step .}
elements.sent-2411-121: After preprocessing, we divide the dataset into two splits.
elements.sent-2411-122: The test set comprises of $1200$ workflows with diverse objectives and domains.
elements.sent-2411-123: We use the remaining workflows as the training data to adapt LLMs via standard supervised fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-124: Note that for each fine-tuning example, the label is a single next-step instead of all remaining steps needed to complete the task.
elements.sent-2411-125: The agent is trained to generate all information in the five-line format described above, including the natural language description.
elements.sent-2411-126: To reduce fine-tuning cost, we opt for the parameter efficient method LoRA~\citep{hu2022lora instead of full fine-tuning, since we have not observed significant performance gain by updating more parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-127: We also follow previous work~\citep{zhao2023tuninglayernormattentionefficient, shen2023cross to fine-tune the layernorms in addition to the LoRA adapters.}
elements.sent-2411-128: Based on empirical observations, we set the fine-tuning epoch to 2, effective batch size to 32, LoRA rank to 64 and $\alpha$ to 128.
elements.sent-2411-129: We use a cosine scheduler with 30 warmup steps and a learning rate of 1e-4.
elements.sent-2411-130: There are multiple design choices for \ouragent that might affect the prediction accuracy, fine-tuning cost, and inference latency.
elements.sent-2411-131: We focus on three aspects and perform detailed ablation studies to find out the optimal modeling and training configurations.

elements.sent-2411-133: Intuitively, the quality of a fine-tuned web agent should be relevant to the quality of the pretained LLM.
elements.sent-2411-134: We identify two axes that are crucial to performance---model architecture and model size---and explore seven open-source LLMs spanning these axes: Llama 3.1 8B~\citep{llama3 , Mistral 7B~\citep{mistral7b , Mixtral 8x7B~\citep{mixtral , Qwen2 7B~\citep{qwen2024 , Qwen2 57B~\citep{qwen2024 , Qwen2.5 14B~\citep{qwen2.5 , Qwen2.5 32B~\citep{qwen2.5 , and Codestral 22B~\citep{codestral .}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-135: We fine-tune these models with 1 billion training tokens and evaluate their performance on the test split of the dataset we collected.
elements.sent-2411-136: Given that many of the evaluated LLMs have a maximum context window of approximately 32K, and the processed DOM can exceed this limit, we divide the DOM sequentially into chunks that fit into the context window.
elements.sent-2411-137: For fine-tuning, we use the chunk containing the correct target, but for evaluation, we use the last chunk since the target's location is not known beforehand.
elements.sent-2411-138: When evaluating at a 32K context window, 25\% of the test data do not have the correct target tag in the DOM, i.e., these tasks are unachievable.
elements.sent-2411-139: Thus, we compute two metrics for evaluation: (1) exact match (EM) measures the model's ability to select exactly the same HTML tag as the ground truth; (2) calibrated exact match (Calibrated EM, or CEM) measures the percentage of correct target predictions where the target tag was present in the truncated HTML DOM, i.e., it is EM on the set of examples where the observation contains sufficient information to complete the task.
elements.sent-2411-140: As we scale the context window, these two metrics converge.
elements.sent-2411-141: We report the performance of different LLMs before and after fine-tuning in Table~\ref{table:llmbackbone .}
elements.sent-2411-142: Notably, for all models, specialized fine-tuning drastically increases the prediction accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-143: Both before and after fine-tuning, the Qwen family demonstrates better EM and CEM across small, medium, and large models.
elements.sent-2411-144: We observe performance gains as model size increases.
elements.sent-2411-145: For example, the calibrated EM for Qwen2 57B is higher than its 7B counterpart.
elements.sent-2411-146: Qwen2.5 32B is also better than Qwen2.5 14B.
elements.sent-2411-147: Mixtral 8x7B outperforms Mistral 7B by a large margin as well.
elements.sent-2411-148: However, fine-tuning larger models is significantly more resource-intensive---while Qwen2 7B can be fine-tuned using 8 H100 GPUs in just one day, Qwen2 57B takes over a week using the same hardware configuration.
elements.sent-2411-149: Larger models also incur longer inference times and require multiple GPUs even at a 32K context length.
elements.sent-2411-150: To balance effectiveness and efficiency, we develop two versions of \ouragent using Qwen2 7B and Qwen2.5 32B, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-151: The Qwen2 7B model offers an optimal balance between prediction accuracy and the costs associated with fine-tuning and inference.
elements.sent-2411-152: Meanwhile, the Qwen2.5 32B model provides stronger performance when we have sufficient computational resources.
elements.sent-2411-153: We evaluate the models with 65K context window to add additional context and increase the rate of solvable tasks (Table~\ref{table:contextwindow ).}
elements.sent-2411-154: On both Qwen2 and Qwen2.5, scaling the context window from 32K to 65K leads to approximately $2\%$ performance boost for Exact Match but approximately $2.5\%$ performance drop for Calibrated Exact Match.
elements.sent-2411-155: We hypothesize that this performance degradation might be due to rotary position embedding~\citep{su2021roformer and the fact that it becomes harder to pick the correct target given twice as many options to choose from.}
elements.sent-2411-156: Besides, we note that using 65K context window increases the inference time by approximately four times in practice.
elements.sent-2411-157: All settings are trained and evaluated with Qwen2-7B-Instruct and 32K context window.
elements.sent-2411-158: Lastly, we are interested in understanding the effect of fine-tuning dataset size on the agent's performance.
elements.sent-2411-159: To this end, we sample our training set without replacement into smaller subsets and fine-tune Qwen2 7B on them.
elements.sent-2411-161: Plotting on a log-linear scale, we observe that there is a roughly 2\% performance boost when we double our dataset size.
elements.sent-2411-162: To sum up, using our proprietary dataset, we study the effect of LLM backbone, context window, and dataset size on the agent performance.
elements.sent-2411-163: We find that (1) scaling parameter count generally improves prediction quality, but the latency and training time of large LLMs can be prohibitive; (2) using longer context window boosts model performance on EM but increases the inference time significantly; (3) training with more tokens is helpful.
elements.sent-2411-164: Based on these insights, we develop two versions of \ouragent: \ouragent-Small, based on Qwen2 7B, and \ouragent-Large, based on Qwen2.5 32B.
elements.sent-2411-165: Both versions are fine-tuned on the full 6B-token dataset at a 32K context window.
elements.sent-2411-166: While \ouragent-Large demonstrates better performance in both internal and external evaluation,s the 7B \ouragent-Small is cheaper to serve at inference time, particularly when compared to large-scale proprietary models.
elements.sent-2411-167: We evaluate \ouragent on three web datasets.
elements.sent-2411-168: We first consider the next-step prediction setting, where performance is evaluated only on a single next step.

elements.sent-2411-195: At each step, the agent is asked to predict a single action, consisting of an operation and the target element.
elements.sent-2411-196: Performance is measured by element accuracy, which checks if the correct target is selected; action F1 score, which measures operation correctness like text input; step success rate, which evaluates whether both the target element and the operation are correct; and task success rate, indicating all steps are correct.
elements.sent-2411-197: The original Mind2Web benchmark reports two sets of baselines: (1) multi-stage, multi-choice question-answering agents (i.e., the MindAct family) first use a pretrained element-ranking model to filter out 50 candidate elements from the full DOM and then use a separate LLM to recursively select an action from five candidates in a multi-choice question-answering (QA) fashion until one action is chosen; (2) a single-stage, generation-based agent (i.e., fine-tuned Flan-T5$_B$) directly generates the operation and the target based on the full DOM.
elements.sent-2411-198: The multi-stage baselines generally show higher metrics than direct generation models, as the element selection process effectively filters out noise, simplifying the task.

elements.sent-2411-204: We evaluate \ouragent on both multi-stage QA and direct generation.
elements.sent-2411-205: For the multi-stage setting, we first use the pretrained Mind2Web element-ranker to obtain the element ranking.
elements.sent-2411-206: Then, given the output of \ouragent, we traverse the sorted list of HTML elements from top to bottom, and stop when the agent's generated HTML element is a subchild of the element.
elements.sent-2411-207: We then replace \ouragent's prediction by the element.
elements.sent-2411-208: For direct generation, we simply compare the output of our agent to the ground truth action and target.
elements.sent-2411-209: We report results following the evaluation procedure specified in the Mind2Web repository in Table~\ref{table:mind2web .}
elements.sent-2411-210: For the multi-stage setting, \ouragent-Large achieves the best overall zero-shot performance.
elements.sent-2411-211: Our element accuracy and step success rate metrics are also competitive with the best fine-tuned baseline, HTML-T5-XL, on cross-website and cross-domain tasks.
elements.sent-2411-212: However, our task success rates are not satisfactory, which is mainly due to the distribution differences between our training data and the Mind2Web data.
elements.sent-2411-213: Upon inspection, we find that the primary failure cases of our models are (1) predicting the subchild element of the ground truth instead of the ground truth; (2) predicting another element with identical function but is different from the ground truth; and (3) our agent tends to decompose type actions into click followed by type actions.
elements.sent-2411-214: In many cases, we actually correctly predict the action description.
elements.sent-2411-215: These situations can be addressed by improving the evaluation procedure, which we discuss later in this section and in Appendix~\ref{appendix:mind2webrefine .}
elements.sent-2411-216: As for direct generation, \ouragent-Large outperforms all existing baselines.
elements.sent-2411-217: Our step success rates are 2-3 times higher than those achieved by the fine-tuned Flan-T5 and show an improvement of 5-10\% over Synapse, which utilizes GPT-3.5.
elements.sent-2411-218: We attribute \ouragent's strong performance to the diversity and high quality of the workflows in our dataset.
elements.sent-2411-219: Relatedly, the three test sets (Cross-Task, Cross-Website, Cross-Domain) are designed to capture different degrees of domain generalization difficulty.
elements.sent-2411-220: Since we do not train on Mind2Web data, the performance of \ouragent is similar across all three test sets.
elements.sent-2411-221: As mentioned earlier, we observe several limitations in the Mind2Web evaluation that underestimate our agent's performance.
elements.sent-2411-222: First, the evaluation strictly compares element IDs, where only the outermost tags are labeled as ground truths, disregarding the subchildren.
elements.sent-2411-223: This leads to inaccuracies when our agent selects functionally identical but hierarchically deeper elements, which are marked as incorrect.
elements.sent-2411-224: Second, for direct generation, \ouragent might select a functionally identical element that is located in a different part of the website (e.g., consider clicking on the next page button vs. clicking on the page number).
elements.sent-2411-225: Lastly, there is a notable discrepancy between the distribution of our training data and the synthetic trajectories of Mind2Web.
elements.sent-2411-226: For instance, Mind2Web expects immediate type actions for textarea or input tags, whereas our model first clicks before typing.
elements.sent-2411-227: To better reflect \ouragent's capabilities, we refine the evaluation method by relaxing the labels to include subchildren of the ground truths.
elements.sent-2411-228: For direct generation, we also introduce an element attribute matching step that compares not only the element IDs but also tag and text attributes.
elements.sent-2411-229: The results with refined evaluation is shown in Appendix~\ref{appendix:mind2webrefine .}
elements.sent-2411-230: We observe an average of 8\% increase in task success rate and element accuracy for \ouragent, showing the need for enhancing evaluation of text-based benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-231: It is worth noting again that results in Table~\ref{table:mind2web follow the original evaluation procedures to ensure fair comparison with established baselines.}
elements.sent-2411-232: While the Mind2Web results are promising, we note that another limitation of static, text-based benchmark is that the ground truth evaluation does not account for different action sequences that could reach the same goal.
elements.sent-2411-233: For instance, to book a flight, one can first enter the destination or first choose the departure date, but the ground truth trajectory only accounts for one possibility.
elements.sent-2411-234: Considering this, we also evaluated \ouragent on a dynamic benchmark WebArena~\citep{zhou2024webarena .}
elements.sent-2411-235: WebArena \citep{zhou2024webarena features 812 web navigation tasks across five domains: E-commerce (OneStopShop), social forums (Reddit), software development (GitLab), content management (CMS), and online map (OpenStreetMap).}
elements.sent-2411-236: Unlike the static Mind2Web, it implements a dynamic environment for agents to interact with and allows for assessing the functional accuracy of action sequences.
elements.sent-2411-237: Since the WebArena environment is implemented to accept only target element IDs specified in the accessibility tree, whereas \ouragent operates on DOM and outputs targets in HTML, we employ GPT-4o to map between the different representations.
elements.sent-2411-238: More generally, we tackle end-to-end task solving by developing a multi-agent system that utilizes GPT-4o to simulate user interactions with \ouragent.
elements.sent-2411-239: Our system contains four stages: (1) objective refinement: user adds details about the task objective to help complete the task; (2) action generation: based on the current website and action history, agent outputs an action suggestion; (3) action execution: user executes the suggested action, e.g., clicking a button; (4) completeness evaluation: user observes the current state and decides whether the task is completed.
elements.sent-2411-240: We apply the above pipeline to solve the WebArena tasks.
elements.sent-2411-241: In stage 3, GPT-4o maps the agent's output in HTML to the accessibility tree format, which is then processed by the WebArena environment.
elements.sent-2411-242: To further improve performance, we allow \ouragent to generate multiple actions in stage 2 and select the one with the highest confidence using majority vote and GPT-4o analysis.
elements.sent-2411-243: Given the large number of tasks in WebArena and the evaluation costs associated with using a multi-agent system, we limited our testing to \ouragent-Small.
elements.sent-2411-244: More details about evaluating \ouragent on WebArena can be found in Appendix~\ref{appendix:webarena .}

elements.sent-2411-263: In this work, we explore how fine-tuning open-source LLMs with high-quality real-world workflow data can benefit developing specialized web agents.
elements.sent-2411-264: We present \ouragent, which consistently outperforms existing methods that prompt proprietary models in various evaluation settings and benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-265: We also provide empirical insights into data processing and model fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-267: The long-context nature of DOMs presents great challenges in adapting LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-268: In the short term, we aim to enable \ouragent to compare and reason over multiple DOM chunks so that its observation is always complete.
elements.sent-2411-269: This might require integrating a memory component, which could also aid in maintaining context or state across interactions to improve multi-step reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-270: Besides, we currently do not incorporate planning into \ouragent, so its output will be directly used as the next action.
elements.sent-2411-271: However, adding better action selection strategies such as Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) could potentially facilitate online planning and exploration, further improving the agent's decision-making processes in complex scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-272: In the long run, we aim to expand \ouragent's capabilities to handle multi-modal inputs and multilingual content.
elements.sent-2411-273: This would significantly broaden its applicability across different linguistic and visual contexts, making it more versatile and robust in real-world web environments.

elements.sent-2411-280: In this section, we provide more details on the tokenizer-based detection method to remove random character strings.
elements.sent-2411-281: The rationale behind our approach is based on the observation that typical English words consist of more than two characters.
elements.sent-2411-282: Assuming the token count is $t$ and the character count is $s$, this means that when $t=1$, $s\geq2$, leading to $\frac{s {t}\geq 2$.}
elements.sent-2411-283: By setting the pruning threshold to 2 and removing tag attributes with $\frac{s {t < 2$, we aim to eliminate strings composed solely of single-character tokens, which are likely to be nonsensical.}}
elements.sent-2411-284: In our actual implementation, we employ this technique only for tag attributes with $s>32$, being more lenient for shorter attributes.
elements.sent-2411-285: To show that this tokenizer pruning strategy is effective and to study the performance across different tokenizers and pruning thresholds, we perform the following experiments.
elements.sent-2411-286: We take three tokenizers from different models: Qwen2-7B-Instruct, Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3, and Meta-Llama-3-8B.
elements.sent-2411-287: For each tokenizer, we vary the pruning thresholds across a set of values: $\{1.5, 1.75, 2, 2.25, 2.5\}$.
elements.sent-2411-288: Note that it is meaningless to study overly small thresholds (e.g., it is impossible to have $\frac{s {t < 1$) or overly large thresholds (e.g., $\frac{s {t < 3$ could result in the loss of meaningful attributes, as many English words contain three letters).}}}}

elements.sent-2411-314: As mentioned in the main text, we improve the Mind2Web evaluation from two perspectives:.
elements.sent-2411-315: Subchild label relaxation: We hypothesize that the distribution gap between our training data for \ouragent and the Mind2Web test set could be due to Mind2Web preferring ancestor/parent nodes in the HTML tree, while \ouragent's training data prefers lower HTML elements.
elements.sent-2411-316: To this effect, we relax the Mind2Web set of positive candidates to include not only the positive candidates, but also their children (direct children and grandchildren).
elements.sent-2411-317: Attribute matching: Direct generation setting enables higher degree of freedom in element selection.
elements.sent-2411-318: To address scenarios where the predicted element has the same function as the ground truth but is in a different location, we enhance the direct generation evaluation by introducing an element attribute comparison step.
elements.sent-2411-319: Rather than merely comparing the node ID of the predicted and the ground truth elements, we also evaluate the tag and text attributes (e.g., the text displayed on a button).
elements.sent-2411-320: If these attributes match, we consider the prediction to be correct as it has identical functionality.
elements.sent-2411-321: Lastly, we note that in Mind2Web, whenever there is a textarea or an input tag, the expected behavior is to directly execute the type action.
elements.sent-2411-322: However, our model is trained to first click on the input element and then perform the type action.
elements.sent-2411-323: Thus, for actions predicted on textarea or input tags, we adjust our model to replace click actions with type actions and then compare with the ground truths.
